summary: Ottawa City Council established a 180-day (six-month) moratorium on data centers in all zoning districts. Mayor Robert Hasty stated the city wants to put guardrails in place to protect residents should a mega data center candidate emerge. The moratorium applies citywide and gives the city time to study the pros and cons of data center development before entertaining any proposals.

City of Forney proposed a new zoning ordinance requiring data centers to obtain a Conditional Use Permit (CUP) in all zoning districts where they would be allowed. The City Council held a public hearing on March 17. The ordinance would formally define data centers and establish specific development regulations including requirements that cooling equipment, generators, and other infrastructure be fully enclosed. The Planning and Zoning Commission recommended approval. Developed after reviewing Urban Land Institute guidelines and regulations from other cities.

Next Generation Land Company proposed a 150,000 sq ft data center on 147 acres in West Finley Township. The township planning commission began updating zoning ordinances in November 2025 to add restrictions on industrial zoning in response to the proposal. On March 12, 2026, residents and members of the Center for Coalfield Justice protested in downtown Washington, PA, as county commissioners held an invite-only meeting with data center developers, chanting 'Our land, our power, our land, our water.' Residents say the community is being left out of the conversation. Part of a broader grassroots resistance movement across southwestern Pennsylvania.

opposition_groups: Coalition to Protect Prince William County
summary: Prince William County Planning Commission voted 5-3 to deny a public facilities review for the proposed 'Diamond Hill Substation' on Casey Lane in Bristow. The 67-acre site behind Amberleigh Station and Silver Leaf Estates would have included a NOVEC substation (9.6 acres) and an adjacent Dominion switching station to power data centers. Commissioners and residents expressed concerns about proximity to homes and sensitive environmental areas including wetlands, as well as constant noise and potential health impacts. NOVEC has 10 days to appeal to the Board of County Supervisors or resubmit with modifications.
